content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 15, 2018

Whitney Barazoto
Executive Director
Oakland Public Ethics Commission
One Frank H. Ogawa Plaza, Suite 104
Oakland, CA 94612

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-18-125

Dear Ms. Barazoto:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request
as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity
provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) !
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

The Oakland Government Ethics Act ("GEA") was passed in 2014 to establish local ethics
laws that mirror and supplement the Act and other state laws. The GEA incorporates by reference
all gift requirements and restrictions in the Act and imposes additional, lower gift limits on Oakland
public officials. The Oakland Public Ethics Commission is charged with ensuring compliance with
the GEA through enforcement, education and advice, and policy development. As part of its duties,
the Oakland Public Ethics Commission provides advice and educational materials to public
servants.

In 2009, the Oakland City Council adopted revised City of Oakland Policy for Receipt and
Distribution of Passes and Tickets ("Ticket Policy") to meet the requirement of Regulation 18944.1
and to describe the governmental purpose for the distribution of tickets or passes to City officials.
The Ticket Policy further provides the procedures for distribution of tickets to and from the City
("City Tickets") and its officials, "by which those tickets or passes will not be considered gifts

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-18-125
Page No. 2

under the Political Reform Act." These procedures include a list of "governmental purposes" for the
distribution of City Tickets, restrictions on the transfer of City Tickets directly from City officials to
third parties, and a requirement for the receiving official to submit electronic Form 802 information
within a certain time period for subsequent approval by the ticket distributing official.

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

1. How is "agency" defined as used in Regulation 18944.1?

As defined in the Act, "local government agency" means a county, city or district of any
kind including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or any
department, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing. (Section
82041.) We note that some local jurisdictions include a definition of "agency" within their ticket
distribution policy.2

2. For tickets to qualify under Regulation 18944.1(d)(1)(C), who within the City can
receive and distribute tickets on behalf of the agency? Can an elected official such as a City
Councilmember, or the Secretary to the City Council, receive and distribute tickets on behalf of
the City, or must City tickets be received by City administration rather than City legislators?

The distribution of tickets or passes by the agency must be made in accordance with a
policy adopted by the agency. While Regulation 18944.1(d)(1)(C) sets forth the requirements for
the agency's written ticket distribution policy, it does not specify who within the agency may
receive and distribute tickets on behalf of the agency. We note that some local jurisdictions have
opted to include within their ticket distribution policies a designated "ticket administrator" for this
purpose.

3. Pursuant to Regulation 18944.1(f), who in the City is responsible for completing the
Form 802? Would tickets qualify pursuant to Regulation 18944.1(d)(1)(C) if a Councilmember
receiving the tickets completes the Form 802 in the absence of anyone within the City completing
or approving the Form?

Regulation 18944.1 requires tickets distributed under the ticket distribution policy to be
reported on the Form 802, but does not specify who bears responsibility for completing the form.
However, Form 802 requires the agency head or his/her designee to verify the report with a
signature.

4. For tickets to qualify under Regulation 18944.1(d)(1)(C), may tickets received by an
elected official for one of the enumerated public purposes of the Ticket Policy be used instead by
members of the official's immediate family in the absence of the elected official who received the
tickets when the family members' uses does not fulfill any public purpose of the City Ticket
Policy?

_______________

2 For example, the City of Los Angeles ticket policy states "'[a]gency' has the meaning in Municipal Code
Section 49.5.2(A). For purposes of this Chapter, each City Council district is a separate agency".

File No. I-18-125
Page No. 3

Under Regulation 18944.1(e), the Commission generally defers to an agency's
determination of whether its distribution of tickets or passes to its employees serves a legitimate
public purpose of the agency and thus complies with subdivision (c) of the regulation. However,
where the official will not be attending the event, and only family members, whose attendance
would not fulfill any public purpose of the agency, would instead attend the event, the tickets would
not qualify under Regulation 18944.1(d)(1)(C). For the exception to apply, the use of the ticket
must further a public purpose.

5. Is there a threshold amount that requires Form 802 reporting or must all tickets
distributed via the policy be reported on Form 802 regardless of the ticket value? For example, if
a Councilmember or City employee receives two tickets, valued at $10 each, from the Department
of Parks and Recreation to attend an event sponsored by the City, must the two $10 tickets be
reported on Form 802?

There is no threshold amount for reporting under Regulation 18944.1, tickets distributed
under the policy must be reported on the Form 802 otherwise the tickets will constitute a gift under
the Act. Accordingly, tickets not reported on Form 802 must be reported as gifts to the official on
the official's statement of economic interest (Form 700) and are subject to the Act's $470 gift limit.

6. How is the value of a luxury suite ticket established when there is no dollar amount
listed on the ticket?

For purposes of Regulation 18944.1, a '"ticket or pass' means admission to a facility, event,
show, or performance for an entertainment, amusement, recreational, or similar purpose."
Regulation 18946(d)(2) defines a ticket as anything that provides admission to an event and for
which similar tickets are sold to the public. The value of a ticket is the face value of the ticket
(Regulation 18946.1.) "'Face value' means the price as offered for sale to the general public
indicated on the ticket or pass, or if that price is not indicated, the price at which the ticket or pass
would otherwise be offered for sale to the public by the operator of the venue." (Regulation
18946(d)(1).) As such, the value of a luxury suite ticket would be the price at which the ticket
would otherwise be offered for sale to the public.

7. If an elected official receives tickets pursuant to the Ticket Policy but fails to adhere to
the requirements in the Ticket Policy, such as Form 802 reporting requirements and prohibitions
on the personal transfer of City Tickets, do the tickets qualify under Regulation
18944.1(d)(1)(C)?

Regulation 18944.1 merely provides an option that an agency may use, that, if applied
properly, eliminates a public official's receipt of a gift under the Act. Where the requirements of
Regulation 18944.1 are not adhered to, tickets would be considered gifts subject to reporting and
gift limits.

8. If a City employee who is not a Form 700 filer receives tickets from the City to a City
sponsored event, does a Form 802 need to be completed by the agency?

The purpose of Regulation 18944.1 was to create an exemption from the definition of "gift"
for certain payments made to accomplish a public purpose. If the procedure is followed, the

File No. I-18-125
Page No. 4

exemption will apply. If the procedure is not followed, the exemption will not apply, and the item
will be considered a gift. However, if the official is not in a decision-making capacity and not
required to file a statement of economic interest (Form 700), the official is not required to report the
gift or subject to the gift limits.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

Zachary W. Norton
By: Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

The distribution of tickets or passes by the agency must be made in accordance with a 
policy adopted by the agency. While Regulation 18944.1 ( d)(l )(C) sets forth the requirements for

receive and distribute tickets on behalf of the agency. We note that some local jurisdictions have 
opted to include within their ticket distribution policies a designated "ticket administrator" for this

3. Pursuant to Regulation 18944.1 (I), who in the City is responsible for completing the

signature. 
4. For tickets to qualify under Regulation 18944.1(d)(1)(C), may tickets received by an 
elected official for one of the enumerated public purposes of the Ticket Policy be used instead by 
members of the official's immediate f amity in the absence of the elected official who received the 
tickets when the family members' use does notfulflll any public purpose of the City Ticket 
Policy? 
2 For example, the City of Los Angeles ticket policy states "'[a]gency' has the meaning in Municipal Code 
Section 49.5.2(A). For purposes of this Chapter, each City Council district is a separate agency".

Under Regulation 18944.l(e), the Commission generally defers to an agency's 
determination of whether its distribution of tickets or passes to its employees serves a legitimate

must further a public purpose.

distributed via the policy be reported on Form 802 regardless of the ticket value? For example, if 
a Councilmember or City employee receives two tickets, valued at $10 each,from the Department 
of Parks and Recreation to attend an event sponsored by the City, must the two $10 tickets be 
reported on Form 802? 
There is no threshold amount for rep011:ing under Regulation 18944.1, tickets distributed 
under the policy inust be reported on the Form 802 otherwise the tickets will constitute a gift under 
the Act. Accordingly, tickets not reported on Form 802 must be reported as gifts to the official on 
the official's statement of economic interest (Form 700) and are subject to the Act's $470 gift limit. 
6. How is the value of a luxury suite ticket established when there is no dollar amount

(Regulation 18946.1.) "'Face value' means the price as offered for sale to the general public 
indicated on the ticket or pass, or if that price is not indicated, the price at which the ticket or pass 
would otherwise be offered for sale to the public by the operator of the venue." (Regulation 
18946( d)(l ). ) As such, the value of a luxury suite ticket would be the price at which the ticket 
would otherwise be offered for sale to the public. 
7. If an elected official receives tickets pursuant to the Ticket Policy but/ails to adhere to 
the requirements in the Ticket Policy, such as Form 802 reporting requirements and prohibitions 
on the personal transfer of City Tickets, do the tickets qualify under Regulation 
18944.1 (d)(l)(C)? 
Regulation 18944.1 merely provides an option that an agency may use, that, if applied 
properly, eliminates a public official's receipt of a gift under the Act. Where the requirements of 
Regulation 18944.1 are not adhered to, tickets would be considered gifts subject to reporting and

The purpose of Regulation 18944.1 was to create an exemption from the definition of "gift"
